els.name: Public Servant — The Gatekeeping Definition
els.sub: S.21 PPC — eleven enumerated categories, the most cross-referenced term in the Code
els.desc: S.21 lists eleven enumerated categories of person who qualify as a 'public servant': military/naval/air force officers, judges, court officers, jurymen/assessors/panchayat members, arbitrators, persons empowered to confine others, officers whose duty is to prevent/report offences or bring offenders to justice, officers of government, and several further categories tied to specific public functions.
els.legal: S.21 is not background vocabulary — it is a GATE that determines which entire branch of the Code applies before any substantive offence analysis begins. A police officer who breaks into the wrong house while executing a lawful arrest warrant must FIRST be classified under S.21 (almost certainly category 7 or 9, officer whose duty is to bring offenders to justice) before S.78's protection for executing a court order, or S.76/79's good-faith mistake defences, can even be reached — the General Exceptions chapter only becomes relevant once the actor's status and duty are correctly identified through this Explanation. The same gating function recurs throughout the Code: S.116/119's enhanced abetment-concealment tiers, the bribery-adjacent offences, and Criminal Force/Assault's aggravated tiers for assaulting a public servant all require S.21 classification as a threshold question, not an afterthought. Courts have repeatedly had to resolve borderline cases — contractors performing government functions, retired officials acting in a residual capacity, employees of statutory corporations — because the eleven categories, though detailed, do not cover every modern administrative arrangement cleanly.
els.cite: S.21 PPC, all eleven categories — official text. Cross-reference S.78 (General Exceptions, court-order execution), S.76/79 (General Exceptions, mistake of fact), S.116/119 (Abetment module, public-servant enhancement tiers).

landmark.holding: Both terms function as universal gates across multiple chapters already built in this app (General Exceptions, Abetment, Forgery, Trade Marks, Currency Notes) rather than being tied to a single landmark case; no single citation captures their cross-cutting role, flagged honestly rather than inventing one.

sq.q: A police officer, executing a facially valid arrest warrant in good faith, breaks into the wrong house due to an address error not of his own making. The homeowner is injured resisting what he reasonably believes is a home invasion. Before any defence analysis begins, what is the FIRST question a lawyer must answer, and why does it matter which chapter the answer routes the case into?
sq.reveal: The first question is whether the officer qualifies as a 'public servant' under S.21 — almost certainly satisfied here under the categories covering officers whose duty is to bring offenders to justice or to execute lawful process. This classification is not incidental: it is what makes S.78 (protection for executing a court order, even a jurisdictionally or factually defective one, where good faith is shown) and S.76/79 (mistake of fact) reachable at all. Without first establishing S.21 status, the officer would have to rely on the much weaker, general-purpose S.76 mistake defence alone, without the specific institutional protection S.78 provides. The order in which a lawyer reasons through this matters: General Explanations first, General Exceptions second — reversing that order risks missing the strongest available defence entirely.
sq.cite: S.21 PPC as the threshold gate to S.78 PPC's court-order protection — General Explanations must be resolved before General Exceptions can be correctly invoked.

sq.q: A government-employed contractor, hired on a fixed-term basis to inspect food safety compliance, is assaulted while conducting an inspection. The defence argues the contractor is not a 'public servant' because they are not a permanent government employee. Apply S.21 and identify which category is genuinely contested here.
sq.reveal: S.21's category covering officers of Government whose duty is to prevent offences, or to protect public health, safety, or convenience, does not require permanent employment status — it is function-based, not employment-status-based. Courts have generally resisted narrow readings that would exclude contractors and statutory-corporation employees performing genuinely public functions, since the protective purpose of enhanced assault penalties (deterring interference with public duties) applies regardless of the formal employment structure. The contested issue is functional: was this person, AT THE TIME, exercising a duty of the kind S.21 enumerates — not whether their employment contract used the word 'permanent.'
sq.cite: S.21 PPC's function-based categories — contested in practice precisely because modern administrative arrangements (contractors, statutory corporations) do not map cleanly onto the statute's enumerated list.

sq.q: A retired judge, now in private practice, is approached and offered a bribe in exchange for using residual influence with former colleagues to affect a pending case. Does S.21's 'every judge' category capture this person at the time of the offence?
sq.reveal: This is a genuinely hard boundary case, not a settled one: S.21's categories are generally understood to apply to a person's status AT THE TIME of the relevant conduct, and a retired judge exercising no current judicial function would likely fall OUTSIDE the 'every judge' category for this specific transaction, however much informal influence they may retain. This does not mean the conduct is unpunishable — it means it would need to be analysed under a different framework entirely (such as general bribery/corruption provisions not tied to current public-servant status), illustrating why getting the S.21 classification wrong doesn't just weaken a case, it can route it toward entirely the wrong statutory framework.
sq.cite: S.21 PPC's status-at-time-of-conduct requirement — illustrating a genuine boundary case where misclassification would route the matter toward the wrong statutory framework entirely, not merely a weaker version of the right one.

sq.q: Why does S.21 list eleven separate categories rather than a single general definition like 'anyone performing a government function'?
sq.reveal: A single broad definition would create exactly the kind of interpretive uncertainty S.21 is designed to avoid — enumerated categories give courts a checklist to apply rather than an open-ended standard to debate from scratch in every case. This reflects a deliberate drafting choice favouring predictability over flexibility: the cost is that genuinely public-function actors in administrative arrangements not contemplated in 1860 (contractors, statutory corporation employees) sit in genuine grey zones, but the benefit is that the vast majority of cases — judges, police, military, court officers — are resolved instantly without argument. The grey zones are real, but they are the deliberate price of avoiding a much larger number of disputes the enumerated approach prevents.
sq.cite: S.21 PPC's enumerated-category structure — a deliberate predictability-over-flexibility drafting choice, with the residual grey zones being a known and accepted cost.

els.dike:
Actor qualifies as 'public servant' if ANY ONE of eleven enumerated categories is met
(military/naval/air officer ∨ judge ∨ court officer ∨ juryman/assessor/panchayat member ∨ arbitrator ∨ ... )
Disjunction, not conjunction — one category alone is enough

els.name: Good Faith — The Universal Gatekeeper
els.sub: S.52 PPC — due care and attention, not mere sincerity
els.desc: S.52: nothing is said to be done or believed in good faith which is done or believed without due care and attention.
els.legal: S.52 is arguably the single most consequential definitional sentence in the entire Code, despite its brevity, because it sets the STANDARD that every good-faith-dependent provision elsewhere silently incorporates: S.76/79 mistake of fact, S.78 court-order execution, S.88/89/92's consent-based medical exceptions, and the reverse-burden defences in Forgery (S.482, S.486) and Currency Notes (S.489F) all turn on whether the actor's belief was held in 'good faith' as S.52 defines it — and S.52 sets an OBJECTIVE standard (due care and attention), not a subjective one (genuine sincerity). This is the single most commonly misunderstood point by students and, in practice, by inexperienced counsel: a defendant who genuinely, sincerely believed something, but arrived at that belief carelessly, has NOT acted in good faith under S.52, and every downstream defence collapses as a result. The consequence of getting this wrong is binary and total — full acquittal versus full conviction — making S.52 the highest-stakes single definitional term in the Code for outcome severity, even though it appears in the statute less often than purely mechanical terms.
els.cite: S.52 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.76/79, S.78, S.88/89/92 (General Exceptions, all built), S.482/486 (Forgery, Trade Marks modules), S.489F (Currency Notes module) — all silently incorporate this same objective due-care standard.

sq.q: Returning to the wrong-address raid: the officer genuinely and sincerely believed he had the correct address, but it later emerges he never actually checked the warrant against the door number, relying instead on a colleague's verbal say-so from two days earlier. Apply S.52. Does the officer's sincere belief satisfy 'good faith'?
sq.reveal: No. S.52's test is objective — due care and attention — not subjective sincerity. However genuinely the officer believed he had the right address, failing to verify the warrant against the actual door number before forcing entry is a failure of due care, not merely an innocent mistake. This single finding can collapse the entire defence: without good faith established, S.78's protection for executing even a defective court order (which itself requires good-faith belief in jurisdiction/correctness) becomes unavailable, and the officer is left relying on weaker, more general arguments. This is precisely why S.52 functions as a gate rather than a formality — the same sincere officer succeeds or fails entirely depending on one fact: did he check.
sq.cite: S.52 PPC's objective due-care standard — sincere but careless belief fails the test, with the consequence that an entire downstream defence (here, S.78) becomes unavailable.
Criminal Laws (Amendment) Act, 2023 (S.52B inserted after S.52A, PPC 1860) — Senate of Pakistan
Criminal Laws (Amendment) Act, 2023 (S.52C inserted after S.52B, PPC 1860) — Senate of Pakistan
Criminal Laws (Amendment) Act, 2023 (S.52D inserted after S.52C, PPC 1860) — Senate of Pakistan

sq.q: A surgeon performs emergency treatment on an unconscious patient without consent, genuinely believing the procedure necessary, but bypasses the hospital's own standard pre-operative verification checklist due to time pressure that, on review, was not as severe as the surgeon believed. Apply S.52 and S.92 (emergency action without consent). Is the consent-bypass protected?
sq.reveal: This is a genuinely contestable application, not a clean one: S.92's protection requires the act be done in good faith, and S.52 asks whether due care and attention was exercised given the actual circumstances, not the circumstances as the surgeon mistakenly perceived them. If a court finds the time pressure was objectively less severe than believed, and that following the standard checklist remained genuinely feasible, the surgeon's belief that bypassing it was necessary may fail the due-care standard despite being sincerely held — illustrating that S.52's objective standard can defeat even a textbook-sympathetic defendant (a doctor acting to save a life) if the care-and-attention element is not independently satisfied.
sq.cite: S.52 PPC applied to S.92 PPC (General Exceptions, emergency consent) — even sympathetic, life-saving conduct can fail the objective good-faith standard if due care was not independently exercised.

sq.q: How does S.52's objective 'due care and attention' standard differ from a pure negligence standard in civil law, and why does this distinction matter for a defendant relying on a General Exception?
sq.reveal: S.52 is narrower and more forgiving than a full negligence inquiry: it asks only whether SOME meaningful care and attention was exercised in forming the relevant belief, not whether the defendant met the standard of a reasonably prudent person in all respects, nor whether a better-informed actor could have done more. This matters practically because it means a defendant does not need to prove perfection or best practice — only that the belief was not formed carelessly or recklessly. The distinction is genuinely important for defence strategy: arguing 'my client could have done more' (a civil-negligence framing) is the wrong target; the actual question is narrower — was there a genuine, attentive basis for the belief at all, however imperfect.
sq.cite: S.52 PPC's due-care standard — narrower than full civil negligence, asking only whether meaningful care was exercised, not whether the defendant met an ideal standard.

sq.q: Does S.52 require that the belief ultimately turn out to be factually correct, or only that it was carefully formed?
sq.reveal: Only carefully formed — S.52 says nothing about the belief's ultimate correctness. This is the entire functional purpose of good-faith-based defences throughout the Code: they protect people who, despite exercising genuine care, were nonetheless factually wrong, which is precisely the wrong-address-raid scenario in its clean form. If good faith required actual correctness, S.76/79/78/88/89/92 would add nothing beyond a simple 'were you right' test — the entire point of incorporating S.52's standard is to protect careful-but-mistaken actors, distinguishing them from careless ones, regardless of outcome.
sq.cite: S.52 PPC — protects careful-but-mistaken belief regardless of ultimate factual correctness, which is the entire functional purpose of every good-faith-dependent exception built on top of it.

els.dike:
Good faith ≡ ~(done without due care and attention)
Due care ∧ due attention required — honest belief alone is NOT enough if careless

els.name: Dishonestly vs Fraudulently — The Property-Offence Mens Rea Gate
els.sub: S.24/S.25 PPC — wrongful gain/loss vs deception, the line between Theft and Cheating
els.desc: S.24: 'dishonestly' means doing something with intent to cause wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another. S.25: 'fraudulently' means doing something with intent to deceive.
els.legal: This pairing is the single most consequential mens rea fork in the entire property-offences chapter: which word a section uses determines which offence is even available to charge, and the two concepts are NOT interchangeable despite sounding similar. Dishonesty (S.24) is purely about gain/loss — it requires NO deception of anyone; a person who simply takes property knowing it isn't theirs, with no lie told to anyone, is dishonest but not fraudulent. Fraud (S.25) is purely about deception — it requires NO wrongful gain or loss to be intended; a person who deceives someone purely to embarrass them, with no property motive at all, is fraudulent but not dishonest. Theft (S.378) requires only dishonest intention. Cheating (S.415) requires fraudulent or dishonest inducement plus deception. Many Forgery and Criminal Misappropriation provisions hinge on which standard the prosecution can actually prove on the facts — getting this fork wrong at the charging stage can mean prosecuting under a section the facts don't support.
els.cite: S.24 and S.25 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.378 PPC (Theft module), S.415 PPC (Cheating module), S.403 PPC (Misappropriation module) — each keyed to one or both of these standards.

sq.q: A finds a wallet on the street with no identifying information, decides on the spot to keep it rather than attempt to locate the owner, and tells no one any lie in the process. Apply S.24 and S.25. Is A's conduct dishonest, fraudulent, both, or neither?
sq.reveal: Dishonest, not fraudulent. A intends wrongful gain to himself (or wrongful loss to the true owner) by keeping the wallet — satisfying S.24 directly. No deception of any person occurred — A told no lies and induced no one to believe anything false — so S.25's fraud standard is not satisfied. This is the clean illustration of the fork: gain/loss without deception is dishonesty alone, which is sufficient for Criminal Misappropriation (S.403) but would NOT support a Cheating charge, which requires deception.
sq.cite: S.24 PPC satisfied (wrongful gain/loss) without S.25 PPC (no deception occurred) — illustrating the two standards are independently triggered.

sq.q: B tells a deliberate lie to C purely to make C look foolish in front of others, with no intention of gaining any property or causing C any financial loss whatsoever. Apply S.24 and S.25. Is B's conduct dishonest, fraudulent, both, or neither?
sq.reveal: Fraudulent, not dishonest. B intends to deceive C — satisfying S.25 directly. But B has no intent to cause wrongful gain to anyone or wrongful loss to anyone — satisfying nothing under S.24. This confirms the fork runs both directions: deception without a property-gain/loss motive is fraud alone, which matters because several PPC provisions (e.g., certain forgery-adjacent offences) trigger on fraudulent intent even where no property changes hands.
sq.cite: S.25 PPC satisfied (deception) without S.24 PPC (no wrongful gain/loss intended) — confirming the fork operates symmetrically.

sq.q: D induces E, through a deliberate lie about a product's quality, to sell D the product at a price far below its true value. Apply S.24 and S.25 together. Why would a prosecutor likely charge this under Cheating (S.415) rather than Theft (S.378), even though dishonest gain is clearly present?
sq.reveal: D's conduct satisfies BOTH standards simultaneously: dishonest intent (wrongful gain to D / wrongful loss to E) AND fraudulent deception (the lie about quality). Cheating (S.415) specifically requires fraudulent OR dishonest inducement through deception — and is the more precise charge here because the wrongful gain was achieved THROUGH deception of E's own decision-making, which is structurally different from Theft's requirement of taking property without the owner's consent. Theft does not fit because E voluntarily, if deceptively induced, agreed to the sale; Cheating is the section built specifically for gain achieved via induced consent.
sq.cite: S.24 and S.25 PPC jointly satisfied — Cheating (S.415 PPC, Cheating module) is the structurally correct charge specifically because gain was achieved through deception-induced consent, not non-consensual taking.

sq.q: Why might the legislature have defined two separate, independently-triggerable mental states (dishonesty and fraud) rather than a single combined 'dishonest and fraudulent' standard for all property offences?
sq.reveal: Separating the two standards lets each offence in the property chapter be precisely calibrated to the specific wrong it targets: Theft (taking without consent) only needs a gain/loss motive, since no deception of the victim is involved in the taking itself; Cheating (gain through induced consent) specifically needs deception, since the wrong lies in corrupting the victim's own decision-making. A single combined standard would either over-criminalise simple non-deceptive dishonesty as if it required proving deception, or under-capture pure deception schemes with no direct property-gain motive. The two-standard structure lets the legislature match each offence's mental element precisely to its actus reus.
sq.cite: S.24 PPC and S.25 PPC as independently calibrated standards — precisely matched to each offence's distinct actus reus (non-consensual taking vs deception-induced consent) rather than a single blended test.

els.dike:
Dishonestly (S.24): intent to cause wrongful gain ∨ wrongful loss
Fraudulently (S.25): intent to deceive
Parallel definitions, not synonyms — an act can be one without the other

els.name: Reason to Believe — The Objective Evidentiary Standard
els.sub: S.26 PPC — sufficient cause to believe, not certainty and not mere suspicion
els.desc: S.26: a person is said to have 'reason to believe' a thing if they have sufficient cause to believe that thing, and not otherwise.
els.legal: S.26 sets a deliberately calibrated middle standard, and the calibration matters enormously in practice: it is LOWER than actual knowledge (the person need not be certain or have direct proof) but HIGHER than mere suspicion or curiosity (idle doubt or a passing hunch does not satisfy it). This is the recurring evidentiary threshold in Receiving Stolen Property (S.410-414, where 'reason to believe' the property was stolen is the core mental element), several Abetment provisions, and reverse-burden defences across Forgery and Currency Notes already built elsewhere in this app. The standard is OBJECTIVE — sufficient cause judged by what a reasonable person in the defendant's position would have inferred from the circumstances actually known to them, not by what the defendant subjectively chose to believe or disbelieve.
els.cite: S.26 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.410-414 (Receiving Stolen Property module) — the clearest example of 'reason to believe' as the entire operative mental element of an offence.

sq.q: A buys a high-value watch from a stranger on the street at a fraction of its retail price, with the seller visibly nervous and unable to produce any receipt or explanation of ownership. A asks no questions and completes the purchase. The watch is later found to be stolen. Apply S.26. Does A have 'reason to believe' the watch was stolen?
sq.reveal: Likely yes. The circumstances — a stranger, an implausibly low price, visible nervousness, and complete absence of ownership documentation — collectively supply sufficient cause for a reasonable person to suspect the property was stolen, even though A never asked and was never directly told. A's choice not to inquire does not avoid the standard; S.26 asks what the circumstances objectively supplied reason to believe, not what A subjectively decided to find out.
sq.cite: S.26 PPC's objective standard — sufficient cause supplied by the surrounding circumstances, regardless of whether the defendant chose to actively avoid confirming it.

sq.q: B buys a used phone from a registered second-hand electronics shop, at a normal market price, with a standard receipt issued. The phone is later found to have been stolen by an employee of the shop without the shop owner's knowledge. Does B have 'reason to believe' the phone was stolen?
sq.reveal: No. Nothing in the circumstances available to B — a legitimate-seeming shop, a normal price, a standard receipt — would have given a reasonable person sufficient cause to suspect the property was stolen. This is the standard's lower bound doing its protective work: B's complete lack of suspicious circumstances means the objective threshold is not met, regardless of the fact that the property happened to actually be stolen.
sq.cite: S.26 PPC — the objective standard not met where no suspicious circumstances were actually present, despite the property's true stolen status.

sq.q: Is S.26's 'reason to believe' the same thing as 'knowledge' that a fact is true? If a prosecutor can only prove reason to believe but not actual knowledge, is the case lost?
sq.reveal: No to both — this is the precise point of having a deliberately calibrated middle standard. S.26 explicitly sets a LOWER bar than knowledge: the defendant need not have known for certain, only had sufficient cause to believe. A prosecutor who can establish the objective circumstantial basis for belief succeeds under S.26-based provisions even without proving the defendant's subjective certainty — this is precisely why 'reason to believe' rather than 'knowledge' is the operative standard in Receiving Stolen Property and similar provisions, since requiring proof of actual certainty would make such offences nearly impossible to prosecute.
sq.cite: S.26 PPC deliberately set below the knowledge standard — enabling prosecution based on objectively sufficient circumstantial cause without requiring proof of subjective certainty.

sq.q: How does S.26's 'sufficient cause to believe, and not otherwise' final clause function, and why might the drafters have added it explicitly?
sq.reveal: The 'and not otherwise' clause is a deliberate boundary-setting device, foreclosing any argument that a lower threshold — mere suspicion, curiosity, or a vague unease — could satisfy the standard. By stating the standard positively (sufficient cause) AND negatively (not otherwise), the section closes off attempts to stretch 'reason to believe' downward toward something closer to bare speculation, which would make the standard unfairly easy for prosecutors to satisfy and erode the protective floor for genuinely innocent purchasers and recipients.
sq.cite: S.26 PPC's 'and not otherwise' clause — explicitly foreclosing dilution of the standard toward mere suspicion or speculation.

els.name: Common Intention — Cross-Reference Note
els.sub: S.34 PPC — full doctrinal treatment lives in the dedicated Common Intention module
els.desc: S.34: when a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each is liable for that act as if done by him alone. Full 8-element treatment — the foundational rule, prior-concert requirement, the Common Intention vs Common Object (S.149) distinction, the different-intentions limiting principle, and withdrawal — is built in the dedicated Common Intention module.
els.legal: S.34's textual rarity in the Code (it appears far less often by name than its practical litigation weight would suggest) is itself the key insight: courts do not need to recite 'S.34' for the doctrine to be in play — it attaches silently to whichever substantive offence is charged, wherever several persons acted with a genuinely shared design. This is exactly why frequency-counting alone would undersell its importance, and exactly why this app gives it a full dedicated module rather than treating it as a minor General Explanations entry. If you have not yet worked through the Common Intention module, start there — this entry exists only to confirm the cross-reference and prevent this General Explanations Deep Dive from duplicating that work.
els.cite: S.34 PPC — official text. See the dedicated Common Intention module (8 elements: foundational rule, prior concert, vs Common Object, different-intentions limit, withdrawal) for full treatment.
els.ll: S.34 PPC — official text; full treatment in Common Intention module

sq.q: Why does S.34 sit in the General Explanations chapter at all, rather than being defined as part of a specific offence?
sq.reveal: S.34 is a general-purpose liability-attribution rule, not an offence-specific provision — it can attach to virtually any offence in the Code wherever several persons act in furtherance of a genuinely shared intention. Placing it in General Explanations (rather than, say, within the Murder or Theft chapters) reflects that its function is interpretive and cross-cutting: it tells you HOW to read liability across the whole Code when multiple people are involved, the same structural role the other General Explanations terms play.
sq.cite: S.34 PPC's placement in General Explanations — reflecting its cross-cutting, interpretive function rather than offence-specific application.

sq.q: A student preparing for an exam on Common Intention should look where in this app for the full doctrinal depth — sparring questions on prior concert, the S.149 distinction, withdrawal, and the limiting principle on different intentions?
sq.reveal: The dedicated Common Intention module, not this General Explanations entry. This entry exists specifically to flag the cross-reference and avoid duplicating that work — General Explanations' role here is to confirm S.34's gating function and direct you to the fuller treatment, consistent with the weighting principle of not rebuilding content that already exists elsewhere in the app.
sq.cite: Cross-reference to the dedicated Common Intention module — avoiding duplicate content across modules.

els.name: Voluntarily — The Threshold for Any Actus Reus at All
els.sub: S.39 PPC — causing an effect by means which one had the power of controlling, with knowledge or reason to believe a likely effect
els.desc: S.39: a person is said to cause an effect 'voluntarily' when they cause it by means whereby they had the power of controlling its production, and either intended to cause it, or, by the means employed, knew or had reason to believe it likely to be caused.
els.legal: S.39 is the lowest-frequency-of-actual-dispute but highest-stakes term in this entire tier: it decides whether there is an Actus Reus at all, not merely how severe the offence is. A genuinely involuntary act — true automatism, a reflex, an externally-imposed physical force the person had no power to control — fails S.39 entirely, and the prosecution's case collapses at the threshold, before intention, knowledge, or any defence is even reached. This is conceptually distinct from, and prior to, every Mens Rea inquiry: a person can have had the clearest possible criminal intention and still escape liability if the act causing the prohibited effect was not voluntary in this specific S.39 sense, because intention without a voluntary controlling act is not Actus Reus at all.
els.cite: S.39 PPC — official text. Cross-reference Actus Reus & Mens Rea foundation module (already built) — S.39 supplies the precise statutory definition of the voluntariness this module's general doctrine depends on.

sq.q: A driver suffers a sudden, first-ever, genuinely unforeseeable seizure while driving and strikes a pedestrian, having had no prior history or warning sign of any such medical condition. Apply S.39. Does the driver's loss of control during the seizure satisfy 'voluntarily' for the striking itself?
sq.reveal: Likely not, for the specific moment of the seizure itself. S.39 requires the person to have had the power of controlling the means by which the effect was caused — during a genuine, unforeseeable seizure, the driver had no such power over the vehicle's movement at that moment. This does not necessarily mean the driver faces no liability at all (the decision to drive in the first place, if there had been any known risk, could be analysed separately), but the striking itself, as a discrete act during the seizure, fails the voluntariness threshold S.39 sets.
sq.cite: S.39 PPC — power-of-control requirement not met during a genuine, unforeseeable loss of physical control, defeating Actus Reus for that specific act.

sq.q: Compare the above scenario to a driver who has a known history of seizures, has been advised by a doctor not to drive, and chooses to drive anyway, then suffers a seizure and strikes a pedestrian. Does S.39's voluntariness analysis change?
sq.reveal: Yes, significantly. While the seizure itself remains outside the driver's control in the same way, S.39 also captures effects the person 'had reason to believe likely' through the means they employed — and here, the decision to drive at all, despite known medical advice against it, is itself a controlled, voluntary act undertaken with reason to believe a seizure (and resulting harm) was a real possibility. The relevant voluntary act shifts from the moment of striking to the earlier decision to drive, which the driver did have full power to control and reason to believe was risky.
sq.cite: S.39 PPC's 'reason to believe likely' limb — shifting the voluntariness analysis to an earlier controlled decision (driving despite known risk) where the immediate act (the seizure) is itself involuntary.

sq.q: Why is S.39's voluntariness threshold analytically PRIOR to any Mens Rea inquiry, rather than simply another factor weighed alongside intention?
sq.reveal: Mens Rea questions (did the person intend this, did they know this was likely) only become meaningful once there is established to be a voluntary act in the first place — intention is a property of an act the person actually controlled, not of something that merely happened to them. A person cannot meaningfully be said to have 'intended' an effect produced by a means they had no power to control; the very concept of intention presupposes voluntariness. This is why S.39 functions as a gate rather than a balancing factor: failing it ends the inquiry before Mens Rea is even reached, rather than simply weakening the case.
sq.cite: S.39 PPC as analytically prior to Mens Rea — intention presupposes a voluntary controlling act, making S.39 a threshold gate rather than one factor among several.

sq.q: Does S.39 require the person to have intended the SPECIFIC effect that occurred, or is it satisfied more broadly?
sq.reveal: S.39's text is disjunctive: voluntariness is satisfied if the person either intended to cause the effect, OR knew or had reason to believe, by the means employed, that the effect was a LIKELY consequence. This means S.39 does not require pinpoint intent to the exact outcome — a person who employs a means they have reason to believe is likely to produce a given effect satisfies voluntariness for that effect even without specifically intending it, which is the same broader logic already seen in S.300's lower mens rea clauses for murder (knowledge of imminently dangerous acts).
sq.cite: S.39 PPC's disjunctive structure — intention OR reason to believe likely, mirroring the broader knowledge-based liability already seen in S.300 PPC's lower mens rea clauses (Murder module).

els.dike:
Voluntarily ≡ power of controlling the effect's production ∧ (intended to cause it ∨ knew/had reason to believe it likely)
Conjunction of control with a disjunction of mental states — either intention or foresight satisfies the second limb

els.name: Illegal / Legally Bound to Do — The Omission-Liability Boundary
els.sub: S.43 PPC — defines both the negative (illegal) and positive (legally bound) sides of duty
els.desc: S.43: the word 'illegal' applies to everything which is an offence, or which is prohibited by law, or which furnishes ground for a civil action; a person is said to be 'legally bound to do' whatever it is illegal in him to omit.
els.legal: S.43's two halves do separate, equally important work. The 'illegal' half is BROADER than 'offence' — it sweeps in civil wrongs and merely prohibited conduct, not only criminal offences, which matters because several PPC provisions (the Wrongful Restraint exception, several abetment provisions) turn on whether conduct was 'illegal' in this wider sense. The 'legally bound to do' half is the precise mirror-image definition that makes OMISSION liability coherent at all: a person is only legally bound to do something if failing to do it would itself be illegal — this is the exact term your wrong-address-raid scenario implicates directly, since whether an officer was 'legally bound' to execute the warrant (and thus protected if executing it carefully) versus merely permitted to is the textual hook S.76/78 actually rely on.
els.cite: S.43 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.76 PPC (General Exceptions, mistake of fact, 'bound by law'), S.118 PPC (Abetment module, illegal omission as concealment).

sq.q: Returning to the wrong-address raid: was the officer 'legally bound' under S.43 to execute the arrest warrant, or merely permitted/authorised to do so? Why does this distinction matter for which General Exception applies?
sq.reveal: An officer executing a validly issued warrant is generally 'legally bound to do' so under S.43 — failing to execute a valid warrant without lawful excuse would itself be a dereliction of legal duty, satisfying the 'illegal to omit' test that defines 'legally bound.' This matters because S.76's mistake-of-fact defence specifically protects a person who believes, in good faith, that they are BOUND by law to do an act — a person who was merely permitted (not bound) would need to rely on a different, often weaker, defence. The S.43 classification therefore determines which specific limb of S.76 is even available.
sq.cite: S.43 PPC's 'legally bound to do' definition — directly determining whether S.76 PPC's bound-by-law limb (rather than its weaker justified-by-law limb) is available to the officer.

sq.q: A person breaches a private contract, causing the other party grounds for a civil lawsuit but committing no criminal offence and violating no specific statutory prohibition. Is this conduct 'illegal' under S.43?
sq.reveal: Yes. S.43's definition of 'illegal' explicitly extends to anything that 'furnishes ground for a civil action' — this is deliberately broader than 'offence.' A purely civil wrong, with no criminal dimension at all, still satisfies S.43's 'illegal' standard, which matters wherever a PPC provision uses 'illegal' rather than the narrower 'offence' (for example, in defining an illegal omission for Abetment-by-concealment purposes under S.118).
sq.cite: S.43 PPC's 'illegal' definition — deliberately broader than 'offence,' extending to purely civil wrongs that furnish grounds for civil action.

sq.q: A bystander witnesses a stranger drowning and does not attempt a rescue, despite having no special relationship to the victim (not a parent, lifeguard, or person who created the danger). Is the bystander's failure to act an 'illegal omission' under S.43?
sq.reveal: Generally no, under Pakistani law as structured. S.43 ties 'legally bound to do' to whether OMITTING the act would itself be illegal — and a stranger with no special duty relationship generally has no PRE-EXISTING legal duty to rescue, meaning the omission, however morally troubling, does not satisfy S.43's illegality test. This contrasts sharply with a parent failing to feed a child, or a police officer failing to execute a warrant, where a specific legal duty (parental, statutory, or official) already exists, making the omission illegal under S.43's mirror-definition.
sq.cite: S.43 PPC — illegal omission requires a pre-existing legal duty; a stranger's failure to rescue, absent such a duty, does not satisfy the standard, unlike a parent's or officer's failure within a recognised duty relationship.

sq.q: Why does S.43 define 'legally bound to do' through the negative concept of illegal omission, rather than directly listing categories of legal duty?
sq.reveal: This mirrors the same structural logic seen in good-faith and voluntariness definitions throughout this chapter: rather than attempting an exhaustive, necessarily incomplete list of every duty-creating relationship (which would inevitably miss categories the drafters didn't anticipate), S.43 ties the existence of a duty to the separately-defined concept of illegality, allowing the duty question to be answered by reference to whatever other law (criminal, civil, or statutory) actually creates the relevant obligation, rather than requiring this single section to enumerate every possible duty relationship itself.
sq.cite: S.43 PPC's negative-definition structure — avoiding an exhaustive, inevitably incomplete list of duty-creating relationships by tying the duty question to the separately-defined concept of illegality.

els.dike:
'Illegal' ≡ (an offence ∨ prohibited by law ∨ furnishes ground for a civil action)
'Legally bound to do' ≡ omission of it would be illegal (same three-way test, applied to omission)

els.name: Injury — The Broadest Harm Concept in the Code
els.sub: S.44 PPC — harm to body, mind, reputation, or property
els.desc: S.44: the word 'injury' denotes any harm whatever illegally caused to any person, in body, mind, reputation, or property.
els.legal: S.44's deliberately broad, four-category definition (body, mind, reputation, property) is the harm concept that Criminal Intimidation (S.503's threat-of-injury structure), Hurt's proportionality analysis, and Private Defence's S.99 limitation (no more harm than necessary) all silently incorporate. The critical practical point is that 'injury' under the PPC is NOT limited to physical harm — purely psychological harm (mind), purely reputational harm (a threat to expose a damaging truth or falsehood), and purely economic/property harm each independently qualify, meaning a threat need not involve any physical violence at all to constitute criminal intimidation through threatened 'injury.' The word 'illegally' within the definition is also load-bearing: harm lawfully caused (a court-ordered fine, a lawful arrest causing reputational embarrassment) does not count as 'injury' for these purposes, however much actual harm results.
els.cite: S.44 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.503 PPC (Criminal Intimidation module, threat of injury), S.99 PPC (General Exceptions, Private Defence proportionality limit).

sq.q: A threatens to publicly reveal a true but deeply embarrassing fact about B unless B pays A money, with no threat of physical violence involved at all. Apply S.44 and S.503. Does this threat involve 'injury' capable of grounding Criminal Intimidation?
sq.reveal: Yes. S.44's definition explicitly includes harm to 'reputation' as one of its four independent categories — physical harm is not required. A's threat to damage B's reputation, even through revealing a true fact, can satisfy the injury element of Criminal Intimidation (S.503) if the other elements (intent to cause alarm or compel payment) are also met, since reputational harm is given equal statutory standing to physical harm under S.44's broad definition.
sq.cite: S.44 PPC's reputation category — equally sufficient as physical harm to satisfy the injury element of Criminal Intimidation (S.503 PPC, Criminal Intimidation module), without requiring any threat of violence.

sq.q: A court lawfully sentences C to pay a substantial fine following a fair conviction, causing C genuine and significant financial harm. Does this financial harm constitute 'injury' under S.44 for purposes of any claim C might make against the judge or prosecutor?
sq.reveal: No. S.44's definition requires the harm be 'illegally caused' — a lawfully imposed, court-ordered fine following due process is not illegally caused, however much actual financial harm C experiences. This is precisely why the word 'illegally' is load-bearing within the definition: it excludes the entire category of harm that flows from the lawful operation of the legal system itself, distinguishing genuine 'injury' in the S.44 sense from harm that is simply the lawful consequence of legitimate legal process.
sq.cite: S.44 PPC's 'illegally caused' qualifier — excluding harm that is the lawful consequence of legitimate legal process, however substantial the actual harm experienced.

sq.q: D, exercising the right of private defence against an attacker, causes harm significantly beyond what was necessary to repel the attack. Apply S.44 and S.99 together. How does S.44's broad injury concept interact with S.99's proportionality limitation?
sq.reveal: S.99 caps the right of private defence at causing no MORE harm than is necessary for the purpose of defence — and 'harm' here draws on S.44's broad concept, meaning the proportionality analysis is not limited to comparing physical injury alone; it can in principle extend to comparing the totality of harm (body, mind, reputation, or property) inflicted against what was genuinely necessary. D's excess harm, evaluated against this broad S.44 standard, would exceed the right of private defence under S.99 if it surpassed what was necessary, regardless of which of the four S.44 categories the excess harm falls into.
sq.cite: S.44 PPC's broad injury concept informing S.99 PPC's proportionality cap (General Exceptions, Private Defence Limitations) — the necessity analysis is not confined to physical harm alone.

sq.q: Why might the legislature have defined 'injury' so broadly across four distinct categories rather than limiting the Code's harm-based offences to physical harm alone?
sq.reveal: A physical-harm-only definition would leave significant categories of serious, real harm — psychological terror, reputational destruction, financial ruin — entirely outside the reach of harm-based offences like Criminal Intimidation, despite these forms of harm often being just as devastating to a victim as physical injury, and frequently being the PRIMARY tool of coercion in extortion and intimidation schemes specifically because they avoid the more easily punished and more easily proven category of physical violence. The broad four-category definition ensures the Code's harm-based protections are not circumvented simply by choosing a non-physical method of inflicting serious harm.
sq.cite: S.44 PPC's four-category breadth — preventing circumvention of harm-based offences through non-physical (but equally serious) methods of coercion, particularly relevant to intimidation and extortion schemes.

els.dike:
Injury ≡ harm to (body ∨ mind ∨ reputation ∨ property)
Four-way disjunction — the broadest harm concept in the Code, any one limb suffices

els.name: Document
els.sub: S.29 PPC — any matter expressed/described upon any substance by marks, used as evidence
els.desc: S.29: 'document' denotes any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures, or marks, intended to be used as evidence of that matter.
els.legal: S.29's definition is intentionally substance-neutral — paper is not required, only that marks be made on SOME substance with evidentiary intent. This load-bears the entire Forgery chapter (already built), where what counts as a 'document' capable of being forged determines the chapter's outer boundary.
els.cite: S.29 PPC — official text. Cross-reference Forgery module (S.463-477A, already built).

sq.q: A makes identifying marks on a wooden plank, intended to later serve as evidence of a property boundary agreement. Is the plank a 'document' under S.29?
sq.reveal: Yes. S.29 is substance-neutral — 'upon any substance' — requiring only marks expressing matter with evidentiary intent, not paper specifically. The plank qualifies, which matters for the outer boundary of what can be forged under the Forgery chapter.
sq.cite: S.29 PPC's substance-neutral definition — extending beyond paper to any substance bearing evidentiary marks.

els.name: Valuable Security
els.sub: S.30 PPC — a document creating, extending, transferring, or extinguishing a legal right
els.desc: S.30: 'valuable security' denotes a document which is, or purports to be, a document whereby any legal right is created, extended, transferred, restricted, extinguished, or released, or whereby any person acknowledges that they lie under legal liability or have not a certain legal right.
els.legal: This is narrower than 'document' (S.29) — it specifically requires the document to operate on legal RIGHTS, which is why forging a valuable security (S.467, life imprisonment tier) is punished more severely than forging an ordinary document (S.465) within the already-built Forgery module.
els.cite: S.30 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.467 PPC vs S.465 PPC (Forgery module) — the severity differential this distinction drives.

sq.q: Why does forging a valuable security (S.467) carry a far more severe penalty than forging an ordinary document (S.465), and how does S.30's definition explain the gap?
sq.reveal: S.30 requires the document operate on actual legal rights — creating, transferring, or extinguishing them — meaning a forged valuable security can directly corrupt someone's legal entitlements (property deeds, promissory notes), a more severe harm than forging a document with no direct rights-effect, justifying the Forgery module's tiered severity.
sq.cite: S.30 PPC's rights-based definition explaining the severity gap between S.467 and S.465 PPC (Forgery module).

els.dike:
Valuable security ≡ document whereby a legal right is (created ∨ extended ∨ transferred ∨ restricted ∨ extinguished ∨ released) ∨ document acknowledging legal liability
Six-way disjunction on what the document does to a right

els.name: Moveable Property
els.sub: S.22 PPC — corporeal property of every description, except land and things attached to earth
els.desc: S.22: 'moveable property' includes corporeal property of every description, except land and things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth.
els.legal: This is the entire outer boundary of Theft (S.378, already built) — only moveable property can be stolen under the PPC's structure; immovable property is governed by separate trespass and land-law provisions. The 'corporeal' requirement is itself significant: this is why purely digital data has historically sat outside Theft's reach, a real gap addressed by separate cyber-crime legislation rather than the PPC itself.
els.cite: S.22 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.378 PPC (Theft module) — moveable property is Theft's entire object-boundary.

sq.q: A removes a valuable, free-standing decorative pillar that was merely resting on a property's foundation (not permanently fastened) and carries it away. Is the pillar 'moveable property' capable of being stolen under S.378?
sq.reveal: Likely yes — S.22 excludes things 'permanently fastened,' and a free-standing pillar merely resting (not affixed) would not meet that exclusion, making it corporeal moveable property within Theft's reach, unlike a genuinely fixed structural element.
sq.cite: S.22 PPC's 'permanently fastened' exclusion — narrowly construed, leaving non-affixed items within moveable property even if large or structural-looking.

els.name: Person
els.sub: S.11 PPC — includes any Company, Association, or body of persons, incorporated or not
els.desc: S.11: 'person' includes any Company or Association or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.
els.legal: This extends criminal liability beyond natural human beings to corporate and unincorporated bodies — directly relevant wherever a company is alleged to have committed an offence (e.g. Cheating, Mischief, regulatory PPC offences), and explains why corporate criminal liability is structurally possible under the PPC at all.

sq.q: Can an unincorporated business association, not a registered company, be charged as a 'person' under the PPC?
sq.reveal: Yes — S.11's definition explicitly covers 'any Company or Association... whether incorporated or not,' meaning formal incorporation is not required for the entity itself to be treated as a person capable of bearing criminal liability under the Code.
sq.cite: S.11 PPC — extending personhood to unincorporated associations, not only registered companies.

els.name: Offence
els.sub: S.40 PPC — denotes a thing punishable under this Code, or under any special/local law
els.desc: S.40: 'offence' denotes a thing made punishable by this Code; in several specified sections it also extends to acts punishable under any special or local law.
els.legal: This confirms the PPC's 'offence' concept is not hermetically sealed — certain sections deliberately extend the term to capture special/local law violations as well, which matters for provisions (like several Abetment sections) that reference 'offence' broadly enough to sweep in non-PPC statutory crimes.
els.cite: S.40 PPC — official text. Cross-reference General Explanations S.5 (special laws preserved).

sq.q: An Abetment provision uses the word 'offence' in one of the sections where S.40 extends the term to special/local law. Does abetting a violation of a special law (e.g. a narcotics-control statute) fall within that Abetment provision?
sq.reveal: Yes, where the specific Abetment section is one of those S.40 extends — the offence concept is not limited to PPC-defined crimes alone in those provisions, meaning abetment of a special-law violation can be captured even though the underlying offence sits outside the PPC itself.
sq.cite: S.40 PPC's extension to special/local law — relevant wherever a PPC provision references 'offence' in a section S.40 specifically extends.

els.name: Court of Justice
els.sub: S.20 PPC — a Judge empowered to act judicially alone, or a body of judges so empowered collectively
els.desc: S.20: 'Court of Justice' denotes a Judge empowered by law to act judicially alone, or a body of Judges empowered by law to act judicially as a body, when such Judge or body is acting judicially.
els.legal: The 'acting judicially' qualifier is the operative limit — a judge engaged in purely administrative tasks is not, at that moment, a 'Court of Justice' for PPC purposes, which matters for S.77's judicial-act immunity and S.78's court-order execution protection (both already built in General Exceptions), since both turn on whether the relevant act was genuinely judicial.
els.cite: S.20 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.77/S.78 PPC (General Exceptions, already built).

sq.q: A judge, while handling purely administrative court scheduling unrelated to any judicial decision, makes an error causing harm to a litigant. Does S.77's judicial-act immunity protect the judge for this specific act?
sq.reveal: Not necessarily — S.20 requires the judge be 'acting judicially' for the body/person to constitute a 'Court of Justice' in the relevant sense, and purely administrative scheduling, distinct from judicial decision-making, may fall outside that qualifier, meaning S.77's immunity (tied to judicial acts specifically) may not extend to this particular error.
sq.cite: S.20 PPC's 'acting judicially' qualifier — limiting S.77 PPC's judicial-act immunity (General Exceptions) to genuinely judicial conduct, not all conduct by a judge.

els.name: Structural Reading Rules — S.6, S.37, S.38
els.sub: The invisible scaffolding: how every other section of the Code must be read
els.desc: S.6: every offence definition, penal provision, and illustration is automatically subject to the General Exceptions chapter, even without saying so explicitly. S.37: where an offence requires several acts to be done together, a person who intentionally cooperates in any one of those acts by doing any other act is liable for the offence committed by that cooperation. S.38: persons engaged in the same criminal act may be guilty of different offences, depending on each person's own intention or knowledge.
els.legal: These three rules have zero name-recognition in litigated case law because they are never themselves the subject of a charge — they are the grammar that makes every other section readable correctly, which is precisely why they deserve deliberate attention despite contributing nothing to a frequency count. S.6 means a student should NEVER read an offence definition as if General Exceptions might not apply — it always does, by default, unless the Code says otherwise. S.37 confirms that partial cooperation in a multi-act offence is enough for full liability — a person need not perform every act personally. S.38 is the critical corrective to a common misconception: participants in the SAME criminal episode are not automatically guilty of the SAME offence — each person's liability is assessed by their own individual intention or knowledge, a principle already seen operating concretely in Common Intention's limiting principle (different intentions, different liability) and Abetment's S.110/S.113 divergent-mental-state rules.
els.cite: S.6, S.37, S.38 PPC — official text. Cross-reference Common Intention module's different-intentions limiting principle and S.110/S.113 PPC (Abetment module) — both concrete applications of S.38's underlying principle.
els.ll: S.6, S.37, S.38 PPC — official text

sq.q: A statute defines an offence with no mention of any exception or defence anywhere in its text. Does General Exceptions still apply to a person charged under it?
sq.reveal: Yes — S.6 makes this automatic: every offence definition is read as subject to General Exceptions whether or not the specific section says so. A defendant is never barred from raising a General Exception merely because the charging section is silent about exceptions.
sq.cite: S.6 PPC — General Exceptions apply automatically to every offence definition in the Code, regardless of whether the specific section mentions exceptions.

sq.q: Three people participate in a single violent episode. A struck the fatal blow with clear intent to kill. B held the victim down, aware only that A intended to cause some hurt, not death. C stood watch outside with no awareness of what was happening inside. Apply S.38. Are all three guilty of the same offence?
sq.reveal: No — S.38 explicitly confirms persons engaged in the same criminal act may be guilty of DIFFERENT offences depending on each person's own intention or knowledge. A's clear intent to kill likely supports a murder charge; B's more limited awareness (intent to cause hurt only) may support a lesser charge; C's apparent lack of any relevant knowledge may support no liability at all for the killing itself. The 'same episode' does not produce 'same offence' — this is the same individualised-liability principle already confirmed in the Common Intention module's limiting principle.
sq.cite: S.38 PPC — different offences for different participants in the same criminal episode, based on individual mental state; directly mirrored in the Common Intention module's limiting principle on shared intention.

sq.q: An offence requires two coordinated acts to be completed together — for example, one person distracting a guard while another removes property. A person who only performs the distraction, never touching the property themselves, claims they cannot be liable for the full offence since they didn't personally complete every element. Apply S.37.
sq.reveal: The claim fails. S.37 confirms that intentionally cooperating in even ONE of several acts constituting an offence makes a person liable for the offence as a whole — partial performance of a coordinated multi-act offence is sufficient, not merely a partial defence. The distractor is liable for the full offence, not a lesser version of it.
sq.cite: S.37 PPC — intentional cooperation in any one act of a multi-act offence is sufficient for full liability, not merely partial liability.

els.dike:
S.6: every offence definition → automatically subject to General Exceptions (no need to say so)
S.37: several acts required together ∧ person cooperates in only ONE → still liable for that one act
S.38: several persons ∧ same criminal act ∧ different intentions → each judged on their OWN mental state, not the group's

els.name: Tier 3 — Remaining Light-Note Terms
els.sub: Life, Death, Gender, Number, Man/Woman, Public, Government, Animal, Servant of Government, Wrongful Gain/Loss, Property in Possession of Clerk/Servant
els.desc: S.45 Life: the word 'life' denotes the life of a human being, unless contrary appears from context. S.46 Death: 'death' denotes the death of a human being, unless contrary appears. S.8 Gender: pronouns of one gender include the other. S.9 Number: singular includes plural and vice versa. S.10 Man/Woman: 'man' denotes a male human being of any age; 'woman' a female human being of any age. S.12 Public: includes any class of the public or community. S.17 Government: denotes the Federal Government or a Provincial Government. S.47 Animal: denotes any living creature other than a human being. S.14 Servant of Government: every officer/servant in the service of Government. S.23 Wrongful gain/loss: gain/loss by unlawful means of property to which the gaining/losing person is not legally entitled. S.27 Property in possession of wife/clerk/servant: deemed in the possession of the person on whose account it is held.
els.legal: These terms are bundled deliberately, not as a shortcut, but because the weighting principle established for this module specifically excludes them from heavy treatment: each is genuinely settled, rarely contested in practice, and supplies background clarity rather than gating any downstream chapter or producing a meaningful body of interpretive case law. The one item worth flagging specifically: S.10's definition of 'man'/'woman' applying to 'any age' (not adults specifically) is a small but real point of frequent confusion — it means these terms are NOT age-restricted the way 'major'/'minor' terminology would be, and a PPC provision using 'man' or 'woman' applies regardless of the person's age unless the specific section separately imposes an age requirement (as several offences, e.g. in the Marriage and Women module, separately and explicitly do).
els.cite: S.8, S.9, S.10, S.12, S.14, S.17, S.23, S.27, S.45, S.46, S.47 PPC — official text, bundled.

sq.q: A PPC provision uses the word 'woman' with no separate age qualifier anywhere in that specific section. Does the provision apply only to adult women, or to females of any age?
sq.reveal: Any age — S.10 defines 'woman' as a female human being 'of any age,' with no built-in adult restriction. A provision using 'woman' without a separate, explicit age qualifier applies regardless of the person's age; any age-based limitation must come from the specific offence section itself (as several Marriage and Women module provisions do explicitly), not from the background S.10 definition.
sq.cite: S.10 PPC — 'woman' defined without age restriction; any age-based limitation must be imposed explicitly by the specific offence provision, not assumed from the general definition.
